metadata.title: Effective Go Principles
metadata.description: Comprehensive guide to Go programming principles, patterns, and best practices

pointerVsValueReceiver.title: When to Use Pointer vs Value Receiver
pointerVsValueReceiver.description: Decision tree for choosing between pointer and value receivers for methods

flowchart.question: Does the method need to modify the receiver?
Is the receiver a large struct?
Does the type have other methods with pointer receivers?
Does the receiver contain sync.Mutex or similar non-copyable types?

rules: If any method needs a pointer receiver, all methods should use pointer receivers for consistency
Basic types, slices, and small structs can use value receivers
Maps, channels, and functions are reference types - either works but value is common

sliceVsArray.title: When to Use Slice vs Array
sliceVsArray.description: Decision tree for choosing between slices and arrays
sliceVsArray.recommendation: Almost always use slices unless you need a fixed size for a specific reason
useArray: Size is fixed and known at compile time
Part of a struct where size is semantically important
useSlice: Size is dynamic or unknown at compile time
Need to pass to functions efficiently

newVsMake.title: When to Use new() vs make()
useNew: Allocating any type and getting a pointer to zeroed memory
Creating pointer to struct, int, bool, etc.
newVsMake.rule: make() is only for slices, maps, and channels. Use new() for everything else.

errorVsPanic.title: When to Use Error vs Panic
errorVsPanic.description: Decision tree for error handling strategy
useError: Expected error conditions (file not found, network error, etc.)
Library code - almost always return errors
usePanic: Setup failures that prevent program operation
errorVsPanic.rule: Default to returning errors. Panic only for programmer mistakes or unrecoverable failures.

principles.name: gofmt
principles.definition: Use gofmt to automatically format Go code according to standard conventions. It eliminates debates about formatting and ensures consistency across all Go code.
keyCharacteristics: No line length limit (but reasonable line lengths preferred)
principles.whenToApply: Always. Run gofmt on all Go code before committing.
codeSmells: Manually formatted code with spaces instead of tabs
Inconsistent brace placement
Irregular spacing around operators
Code that doesn't pass gofmt check
principles.refactoringGuidance: Run 'gofmt -w .' to format all files, or integrate gofmt/goimports into your editor to format on save.

principles.name: Commentary
principles.definition: Every package should have a package comment (a block comment preceding the package clause). For multi-file packages, the package comment only needs to be present in one file. Doc comments should be complete sentences starting with the name being declared.
keyCharacteristics: Start with the name being documented
No blank line between comment and declaration
Package comment can be in any file but typically in doc.go or main file
principles.whenToApply: For all exported identifiers (packages, types, functions, methods, constants, variables).
codeSmells: Missing doc comments on exported identifiers
Comments that don't start with the identifier name
Incomplete sentences or fragmented comments
Blank lines between comment and declaration
principles.refactoringGuidance: Add proper doc comments to all exported identifiers. Use tools like 'golint' to identify missing documentation.

principles.name: Package Names
principles.definition: Package names should be short, concise, lowercase, single-word names without underscores or mixedCaps. The package name is part of the identifier when used, so choose names that work well in that context.
keyCharacteristics: Name reflects what package provides, not what it contains
principles.whenToApply: When creating any new package.
codeSmells: Package names with underscores: 'user_service'
MixedCaps package names: 'userService'
Generic names: 'util', 'common', 'helpers'
Redundant names: 'httputil' in 'net/http/httputil' (imports as 'httputil')
principles.refactoringGuidance: Rename packages to be short, single-word, lowercase names. Consider how the package name will appear when imported and used.
principles.badExample:
package http_utils

package HTTPHelper

package common

principles.name: Exported Names
principles.definition: The visibility of a name outside a package is determined by whether its first character is uppercase. Use this to control the API surface and encapsulation.
keyCharacteristics: Lowercase first character = unexported (private to package)
Applies to all identifiers: types, functions, methods, constants, variables
Package name is always lowercase, regardless of export status
principles.whenToApply: When declaring any identifier in Go.
codeSmells: Exporting internal implementation details
Using comments to indicate 'private' instead of lowercase
Inconsistent casing within same category of identifiers
principles.refactoringGuidance: Review all exported identifiers. Make internal implementation details unexported by changing first character to lowercase.

principles.name: Interface Naming
principles.definition: By convention, one-method interfaces are named by the method name plus an -er suffix or similar modification to construct an agent noun: Reader, Writer, Formatter, CloseNotifier etc.
principles.whenToApply: When defining interfaces, especially single-method interfaces.
codeSmells: Interface suffix on multi-method interfaces: 'UserRepositoryInterface'
Redundant 'I' prefix: 'IReader'
Non-behavioral names for interfaces
Names that describe implementation rather than capability
principles.refactoringGuidance: Rename interfaces to describe behavior. For single-method interfaces, use method name + 'er'. For multi-method interfaces, use descriptive names without 'Interface' suffix.

principles.name: MixedCaps
principles.definition: Use MixedCaps or mixedCaps rather than underscores for multi-word names in Go. This applies to constants, variables, functions, and types.
keyCharacteristics: Acronyms are all caps: HTTP, ID, URL
principles.whenToApply: For all multi-word identifiers in Go.
codeSmells: Snake case: 'user_name', 'http_client'
Inconsistent acronym casing: 'HttpClient' (should be 'HTTPClient')
Mixed styles: 'get_userID'
principles.refactoringGuidance: Replace underscores with camelCase. Ensure consistent casing for acronyms (all uppercase).
principles.goodExample:
var userName string
var HTTPClient *http.Client
const MaxConnections = 100

func GetUserID() string { }
principles.badExample:
var user_name string
var Http_Client *http.Client
const MAX_CONNECTIONS = 100

func Get_User_Id() string { }

principles.name: Semicolons
principles.definition: Go's formal grammar uses semicolons to terminate statements, but the lexer automatically inserts them using a simple rule: if the last token before a newline is an identifier, a basic literal, or certain tokens like 'break' or '++', the lexer inserts a semicolon.
keyCharacteristics: No manual semicolons needed in idiomatic Go
Opening brace cannot be on its own line
Automatic insertion based on last token
Semicolons required in for loop clauses
principles.whenToApply: Understanding this rule helps avoid syntax errors with brace placement.
codeSmells: Opening braces on separate lines
Manual semicolons at end of lines
Unexpected syntax errors from newline placement
principles.refactoringGuidance: Move opening braces to same line as declaration. Remove manual semicolons except in for loops.

principles.name: Guard Clauses
principles.definition: Prefer early returns and guard clauses over nested if-else blocks. Handle error cases and edge conditions first, then proceed with the main logic. This reduces nesting and improves readability.
keyCharacteristics: Main logic at lowest indentation level
Clear separation of error handling from happy path
principles.whenToApply: In functions with multiple conditional checks, especially error handling.
codeSmells: Deep nesting of if-else blocks
Happy path buried in nested conditions
Error handling mixed with business logic
Else blocks that could be avoided with early return
principles.refactoringGuidance: Invert conditions and return early for error cases. Eliminate else blocks by returning from the if block.

principles.name: For Loop Patterns
principles.definition: Go has only one looping construct, the for loop, but it can be used in several forms: traditional three-component loop, while-style condition-only loop, infinite loop, and range loop for iterating over slices, arrays, maps, strings, and channels.
keyCharacteristics: Three forms: for init; condition; post {}, for condition {}, for {}
Range form for collections: for i, v := range collection {}
principles.whenToApply: For any iteration need in Go.
codeSmells: Using traditional for when range would be clearer
Ignoring index/value when not needed (should use _ )
Modifying slice while ranging over it (can cause issues)
Not understanding range variable reuse
principles.refactoringGuidance: Use range for iterating over collections. Use blank identifier _ for unused index/value. Be careful with pointers to range variables.

principles.name: Switch Statements
principles.definition: Go's switch is more flexible than in C-family languages. Cases don't fall through by default (no break needed), can have multiple values per case, and can switch on any comparable type or even be expression-less for long if-else chains.
keyCharacteristics: No automatic fallthrough (use 'fallthrough' keyword if needed)
Can switch on any type, not just integers
principles.whenToApply: Use switch for multi-way conditionals instead of long if-else chains. Use type switches for interface type checking.
codeSmells: Long if-else-if chains that could be switch
Unnecessary break statements
Not using multiple values in single case
Verbose type checking instead of type switch
principles.refactoringGuidance: Replace if-else chains with switch statements. Combine multiple case values. Use expression-less switch for complex conditions.

principles.name: Type Switch
principles.definition: A type switch is a construct that permits several type assertions in series. It's like a regular switch but the cases specify types instead of values.
keyCharacteristics: Variable v has the concrete type in each case
Can use _ if variable not needed
principles.whenToApply: When you need to handle an interface value differently based on its concrete type.
codeSmells: Multiple type assertions with if statements
Repeated type assertion of same variable
Not using the type-asserted variable in case block
principles.refactoringGuidance: Replace multiple type assertions with type switch. Use the typed variable directly in each case.

principles.name: Defer
principles.definition: A defer statement pushes a function call onto a stack. The deferred calls are executed in LIFO order after the surrounding function returns, commonly used for cleanup operations like closing files or unlocking mutexes.
keyCharacteristics: Arguments evaluated immediately, but call delayed
Perfect for cleanup and resource management
principles.whenToApply: Use defer for cleanup operations that must happen regardless of how the function exits (normal return, panic, etc.).
codeSmells: Manual cleanup before each return
Forgetting to close resources
Cleanup code duplicated across multiple returns
Defer in loops (can cause memory buildup)
principles.refactoringGuidance: Add defer immediately after resource acquisition. Remove manual cleanup before returns. For loops, consider extracting function to use defer.

principles.name: Multiple Return Values
principles.definition: Go functions can return multiple values, commonly used to return a result and an error. This pattern eliminates the need for in-band error returns (like -1 for error) and makes error handling explicit.
keyCharacteristics: Makes error handling explicit and type-safe
Can return multiple results of different types
Use blank identifier _ to ignore unwanted values
principles.whenToApply: Use for functions that can fail (return result and error) or need to return multiple related values.
codeSmells: Using sentinel values to indicate errors
Returning only error without result
Ignoring errors with blank identifier when they should be handled
Too many return values (>3 suggests need for struct)
principles.refactoringGuidance: Return (result, error) for functions that can fail. Handle or explicitly ignore errors. Consider struct for >3 return values.

principles.name: Named Return Values
principles.definition: Return values can be named and used as regular variables. A return statement without arguments returns the named return values. This can improve documentation and enable 'naked' returns, but should be used judiciously.
keyCharacteristics: Act as variables initialized to zero values
Useful with defer for modifying return values
principles.whenToApply: Use for short, clear functions where it improves documentation. Particularly useful when defer needs to modify return values.
codeSmells: Naked returns in long functions (hard to track values)
Named returns without naked return (unnecessary)
Non-descriptive names like 'r' or 'result'
Overuse leading to unclear control flow
principles.refactoringGuidance: Use named returns sparingly, mainly for documentation or defer interaction. Avoid naked returns in functions >5-10 lines.

principles.name: new vs make
principles.definition: Go has two allocation primitives: new and make. new(T) allocates zeroed storage for a new item of type T and returns its address (*T). make(T, args) creates only slices, maps, and channels, returning an initialized (not zeroed) value of type T (not *T).
keyCharacteristics: make only for slices, maps, channels
principles.whenToApply: Use new for getting pointer to zero value of any type. Use make for slices, maps, and channels that need initialization.
codeSmells: Using new for slices, maps, or channels
Using make for non-reference types
Not understanding difference leads to nil pointer errors
Unnecessary use of new when composite literal works better
principles.refactoringGuidance: Use make for slices, maps, channels. Use composite literals instead of new for structs. Use new only when you specifically need a pointer to zero value.

principles.name: Slices
principles.definition: Slices wrap arrays to give a more general, powerful, and convenient interface to sequences of data. Except for items with explicit dimension like transformation matrices, most array programming in Go is done with slices rather than simple arrays.
keyCharacteristics: Zero value is nil (ready to use with append)
principles.whenToApply: Use slices for almost all sequence/list needs in Go. Use arrays only when size is fixed and known.
codeSmells: Using arrays when slices would be more appropriate
Not checking capacity before appending in loops
Confusing length and capacity
Not understanding slice sharing underlying array
principles.refactoringGuidance: Replace arrays with slices for dynamic data. Use make() with capacity hint for better performance. Be aware of slice aliasing.

principles.name: Two-dimensional Slices
principles.definition: Go can have arrays and slices of arrays and slices. For 2D slices, you must allocate each inner slice independently. This allows for flexible row lengths (jagged arrays) and requires proper initialization.
keyCharacteristics: Each inner slice must be allocated separately
Need to allocate outer slice then each inner slice
principles.whenToApply: When you need matrix-like data structures with potentially variable row lengths, or 2D grids.
codeSmells: Forgetting to allocate inner slices
Assuming rectangular structure without enforcing it
Not initializing properly leading to nil inner slices
principles.refactoringGuidance: Allocate outer slice, then loop to allocate each inner slice. Consider helper function for common 2D slice patterns.

principles.name: Maps
principles.definition: Maps are a reference type that map keys to values. Like slices, maps hold references to an underlying data structure. The key type must be comparable (supports ==). Maps must be initialized with make before use.
keyCharacteristics: Zero value is nil (cannot add to nil map)
principles.whenToApply: Use maps for associative arrays, lookups, and key-value storage.
codeSmells: Not initializing map (using nil map)
Not checking existence before access
Using non-comparable types as keys
Assuming iteration order
principles.refactoringGuidance: Always initialize maps with make(). Use comma-ok idiom to check key existence. Don't rely on iteration order.

principles.name: Printing
principles.definition: Go's formatted printing functions (fmt package) use a conversion syntax similar to C's printf but simpler. %v is the default format, %+v includes field names for structs, %#v prints Go syntax representation, and %T prints the type.
principles.whenToApply: Use appropriate format verbs for debugging and output. Implement String() for custom types to control formatting.
codeSmells: Not implementing String() for custom types
Using wrong format verbs
Overly complex string concatenation instead of formatting
principles.refactoringGuidance: Implement String() method for custom types. Use %+v or %#v for debugging. Use appropriate format verbs.

principles.name: Append
principles.definition: The append built-in function appends elements to the end of a slice. If the slice has sufficient capacity, it is resliced; if not, a new underlying array is allocated. append returns the updated slice, so it's necessary to store the result.
keyCharacteristics: May or may not allocate new array
Can append multiple elements or another slice
Must assign result back to variable
principles.whenToApply: Use append to grow slices dynamically. Prefer pre-allocating with capacity hint for better performance when size is known.
codeSmells: Not assigning append result back to variable
Repeatedly appending in loop without capacity hint
Appending to slice that might be shared/aliased
principles.refactoringGuidance: Always assign append result. Use make() with capacity for known sizes. Be careful with slice aliasing.

principles.name: Initialization
principles.definition: Go provides several ways to initialize values: constants for compile-time values, init functions for complex initialization, and constructors (functions that return new instances). The init function runs automatically before main.
keyCharacteristics: init() runs automatically, once per package
Constructor pattern: New() or NewType() functions
Zero values are usable for many types
principles.whenToApply: Use const for compile-time constants, init for package-level setup, and constructors for complex initialization.
codeSmells: Complex initialization in init (hard to test)
Side effects in init functions
Not providing constructors for types that need validation
Multiple init functions doing unclear things
principles.refactoringGuidance: Keep init simple and side-effect free when possible. Provide New() constructors for types needing validation or setup.

principles.name: Composite Literals
principles.definition: Composite literals construct values for structs, arrays, slices, and maps and create a new value each time they are evaluated. They consist of the type followed by a brace-bound list of elements.
keyCharacteristics: Create and initialize in one expression
Can specify field names (for structs) or just values
Work for structs, arrays, slices, maps
Taking address of composite literal is common pattern
Field names make code more maintainable
principles.whenToApply: Use composite literals for concise initialization of structs, slices, maps, and arrays.
codeSmells: Not using field names for struct literals (fragile)
Verbose multi-line initialization that could be composite literal
Using new() when composite literal is clearer
principles.refactoringGuidance: Use composite literals for initialization. Use field names for struct literals. Take address directly with &T{...}.
principles.badExample:
// Verbose initialization
p := new(Person)
p.Name = "Alice"
p.Age = 30

// Positional fields (fragile, breaks if struct changes)
p2 := Person{"Bob", 25} // What if fields reordered?

// Should use composite literal
var numbers []int
numbers = append(numbers, 1)
numbers = append(numbers, 2)
numbers = append(numbers, 3)

principles.name: Pointer vs Value Receivers
principles.definition: Methods can be defined on pointer receivers (*T) or value receivers (T). Use pointer receivers when the method needs to modify the receiver, when the receiver is a large struct, or for consistency when any method needs a pointer receiver.
keyCharacteristics: Pointer receivers can modify the receiver
Value receivers work on a copy
Pointer receivers avoid copying large structs
Mixing pointer and value receivers on same type is confusing
Go automatically handles & and * for method calls
principles.whenToApply: See decision tree. Generally, use pointer receivers unless you have a good reason for value receivers.
codeSmells: Mixing pointer and value receivers on same type
Value receiver on method that appears to modify state
Value receiver on large struct (expensive copy)
Pointer receiver on small, simple types unnecessarily
principles.refactoringGuidance: Make all receivers pointer or all value for consistency. Use pointer receivers for large structs or when modification needed.

principles.name: Interfaces
principles.definition: Interfaces in Go provide a way to specify behavior. A type implements an interface by implementing its methods - there is no explicit declaration. This enables powerful, flexible, and loosely coupled designs.
keyCharacteristics: Empty interface interface{} (or any) accepts any value
principles.whenToApply: Define interfaces in consumer package, not provider. Use interfaces for abstractions, testing, and polymorphism.
codeSmells: Large interfaces with many methods
Interfaces in provider package instead of consumer
Using interface{} when specific type would work
Premature abstraction with interfaces
principles.refactoringGuidance: Keep interfaces small. Define interfaces where they're used, not where implementations live. Accept interfaces, return structs.

principles.name: Interface Conversions and Type Assertions
principles.definition: Type assertions provide access to an interface value's underlying concrete value. Type switches allow you to compare an interface value against multiple types. Comma-ok idiom safely checks type assertions.
keyCharacteristics: Without ok, panics if wrong type
Can check if interface implements another interface
principles.whenToApply: When you need to access the concrete type from an interface value or check what type is stored in an interface.
codeSmells: Type assertion without comma-ok check
Repeated type assertions instead of type switch
Not handling type assertion failure
principles.refactoringGuidance: Always use comma-ok idiom for type assertions. Use type switch for checking multiple types.

principles.name: Embedding
principles.definition: Go does not provide typical class-based inheritance, but it supports composition through embedding. When a type is embedded, its methods become methods of the outer type, but when called, the receiver is the inner type, not the outer one.
keyCharacteristics: Embedded type's methods promoted to outer type
principles.whenToApply: Use embedding for composition, to add behavior to types, or to create interface combinations.
codeSmells: Deep embedding hierarchies
Embedding when field would be clearer
Confusion about receiver of promoted methods
Overusing embedding instead of explicit fields
principles.refactoringGuidance: Use embedding for 'is-a-kind-of' relationships. Use explicit fields for 'has-a' relationships. Keep embedding shallow.

principles.name: Share by Communicating
principles.definition: Go's concurrency approach encourages 'Do not communicate by sharing memory; instead, share memory by communicating.' Use channels to pass data between goroutines rather than sharing memory with locks.
principles.whenToApply: Prefer channels for communication between goroutines. Use mutexes only when channels don't fit or for protecting invariants.
codeSmells: Excessive mutex usage with shared state
Complex locking patterns
Goroutines sharing variables without synchronization
Using channels when simple mutex would be clearer
principles.refactoringGuidance: Redesign shared memory patterns to use channels. Transfer ownership of data through channels. Use mutexes for protecting simple invariants.

principles.name: Goroutines
principles.definition: Goroutines are lightweight threads managed by the Go runtime. They are cheap to create and have growable stacks. Use the 'go' keyword to launch a function as a goroutine. They enable concurrent execution.
keyCharacteristics: Growable stacks (start small, grow as needed)
principles.whenToApply: Use goroutines for concurrent operations, async tasks, parallel processing, and handling multiple clients/requests.
codeSmells: Creating goroutines without way to stop them (leaks)
Not coordinating goroutine completion
Closure capturing loop variable incorrectly
Too many goroutines (unbounded creation)
principles.refactoringGuidance: Use sync.WaitGroup or channels to coordinate goroutines. Be careful with closures capturing variables. Limit goroutine creation.

principles.name: Channels
principles.definition: Channels are typed conduits through which you can send and receive values with the channel operator <-. Channels can be buffered or unbuffered and provide synchronization between goroutines.
principles.whenToApply: Use channels for communication between goroutines, signaling, and coordination.
codeSmells: Sending on closed channel (panic)
Not closing channels when producer is done
Receiving from nil channel (blocks forever)
Not using directional channels in function signatures
Channel buffer size too large or too small
principles.refactoringGuidance: Use directional channels in function parameters. Close channels from sender side. Use buffered channels to prevent blocking when appropriate.

principles.name: Channel Ranging and Closing
principles.definition: The range keyword works on channels, receiving values until the channel is closed. Only the sender should close a channel. Closing a channel signals that no more values will be sent. Receiving from a closed channel returns zero value immediately.
keyCharacteristics: range on channel receives until closed
Closing is optional (GC will collect unclosed channels)
Comma-ok idiom checks if channel is closed
principles.whenToApply: Close channels when you need to signal completion to receivers. Use range to receive all values from a channel.
codeSmells: Closing channel from receiver side
Closing already closed channel (panic)
Not ranging when receiving all values
Closing channel unnecessarily
principles.refactoringGuidance: Close channels from sender. Use range for receiving multiple values. Use comma-ok for single receives if closure matters.

principles.name: Select
principles.definition: The select statement lets a goroutine wait on multiple channel operations. A select blocks until one of its cases can run, then executes that case. If multiple are ready, it chooses one at random.
keyCharacteristics: Blocks until one case is ready
principles.whenToApply: Use select when waiting on multiple channels, implementing timeouts, or non-blocking operations.
codeSmells: Could use channels directly instead of complex select
Missing default case when non-blocking intended
Not using time.After for timeouts
Select with only one case (just use that channel)
principles.refactoringGuidance: Use select for multiple channels. Add default for non-blocking. Use time.After for timeouts.

principles.name: Error Handling
principles.definition: Go uses explicit error return values for error handling. Functions that can fail return an error as the last return value. Callers should check errors explicitly. This makes error handling visible and forces you to think about failure cases.
principles.whenToApply: Return error from any function that can fail. Always check returned errors.
codeSmells: Ignoring errors with _
Not checking errors
Returning error without context
Using panic for expected errors
principles.refactoringGuidance: Always check errors. Add context when propagating errors. Use fmt.Errorf with %w for error wrapping.

principles.name: Panic
principles.definition: Panic is a built-in function that stops normal execution. When a function panics, it stops executing, runs deferred functions, and returns to its caller. This continues up the stack until all functions in the current goroutine return, then the program crashes.
principles.whenToApply: Use panic only for truly exceptional situations: programming errors, invariant violations, or unrecoverable states. Not for normal error handling.
codeSmells: Using panic for expected errors
Library code panicking instead of returning errors
Panic without good reason
Using recover unnecessarily
principles.refactoringGuidance: Replace panic with error returns for expected failures. Reserve panic for programmer mistakes or impossible situations.

principles.name: Recover
principles.definition: Recover is a built-in function that regains control of a panicking goroutine. Recover is only useful inside deferred functions. During normal execution, recover returns nil. If the current goroutine is panicking, recover captures the value given to panic and resumes normal execution.
principles.whenToApply: Use recover to prevent complete program crash in servers, or to convert panics to errors at package boundaries. Use very sparingly.
codeSmells: Catching all panics and ignoring them
Using recover for control flow
Recovering without logging/reporting
Overuse of recover (indicates too many panics)
principles.refactoringGuidance: Use recover only at strategic boundaries. Always log recovered panics. Re-panic if you can't handle it properly.

principles.name: Error Wrapping
principles.definition: Go 1.13+ provides error wrapping with fmt.Errorf using the %w verb, and errors.Unwrap, errors.Is, and errors.As for working with wrapped errors. This allows adding context while preserving the original error for checking.
principles.whenToApply: Wrap errors when propagating to add context. Use errors.Is for error checking. Use errors.As for extracting error details.
codeSmells: Using %v instead of %w (loses error chain)
Using == to compare errors instead of errors.Is
Not adding context when wrapping
Type assertions instead of errors.As
principles.refactoringGuidance: Use %w when wrapping errors. Use errors.Is for checking specific errors. Use errors.As for extracting error types.

principles.name: Web Servers
principles.definition: Go's net/http package provides a robust HTTP server implementation. The http.HandleFunc registers a handler for a pattern, and http.ListenAndServe starts the server. Handlers should be safe for concurrent execution.
keyCharacteristics: Concurrent by default (each request in goroutine)
principles.whenToApply: Use net/http for building HTTP servers and services. Use middleware pattern for cross-cutting concerns.
codeSmells: Not handling errors in handlers
Blocking operations without timeouts
Not setting proper HTTP status codes
Shared state without synchronization
principles.refactoringGuidance: Use proper error handling in handlers. Set appropriate status codes. Use context for timeouts and cancellation.
